verses.commentary: I speak the truth in Christ, I am not lying, my conscience confirms it through the Holy Spirit. Paul swears by three witnesses: Christ, his conscience, and the Holy Spirit. This solemn oath introduces a painful topic: Israel's rejection of Messiah.

I have great sorrow and unceasing anguish in my heart. Paul's grief for Israel is constant and profound. Love for his people produces deep pain at their unbelief. This is not cold theology but agonizing pastoral concern.

For I could wish that I myself were cursed and cut off from Christ for the sake of my people, those of my own race. Paul would trade his salvation for Israel's if possible (echoing Moses in Exodus 32:32). This is hypothetical (could wish) but reveals his depth of love. His own race emphasizes ethnic solidarity.

The people of Israel. Theirs is the adoption to sonship; theirs the divine glory, the covenants, the receiving of the law, the temple worship and the promises. Israel's privileges are immense: adoption (Exodus 4:22), glory (Shekinah presence), covenants (Abraham, Moses, David), law, temple worship, promises. These are real advantages (3:1-2).

Theirs are the patriarchs, and from them is traced the human ancestry of the Messiah, who is God over all, forever praised! Amen. The patriarchs (Abraham, Isaac, Jacob) are Israel's heritage. The climax: Messiah comes from Israel according to the flesh. Who is God over all affirms Christ's deity. This is worship: forever praised. Amen (so be it).

It is not as though God's word had failed. For not all who are descended from Israel are Israel. Does Israel's unbelief mean God's promises failed? Not at all. Physical descent does not equal spiritual Israel. True Israel is defined by promise, not ethnicity.

Nor because they are his descendants are they all Abraham's children. On the contrary, It is through Isaac that your offspring will be reckoned (Genesis 21:12). Not all Abraham's physical descendants are his true children. Isaac, not Ishmael, was the child of promise. Spiritual lineage matters more than physical.

In other words, it is not the children by physical descent who are God's children, but it is the children of the promise who are regarded as Abraham's offspring. Physical descent does not determine spiritual status. Children of the promise, not the flesh, are counted as offspring. This principle has always governed God's dealings.

For this was how the promise was stated: At the appointed time I will return, and Sarah will have a son (Genesis 18:10). The promise specified Isaac, born supernaturally. God's choice, not human effort, determined the line of promise.

Not only that, but Rebekah's children were conceived at the same time by our father Isaac. A second example: Jacob and Esau, twin sons of Isaac. Same father, same mother, same conception. Yet God chose one over the other.

Yet, before the twins were born or had done anything good or bad, in order that God's purpose in election might stand. Election occurred before birth, before any works. Not by works but by him who calls. God's choice is sovereign, not based on foreseen merit. The purpose: to display God's electing grace.
She was told, The older will serve the younger (Genesis 25:23). God's choice reversed natural expectation. The younger (Jacob) was chosen over the older (Esau). This demonstrates sovereign election.

Just as it is written: Jacob I loved, but Esau I hated (Malachi 1:2-3). Love and hate express God's choice. Hate does not mean emotional hostility but rejection in terms of covenant privilege. Jacob was chosen; Esau was passed over. God's love is elective, not universal in the same way.

What then shall we say? Is God unjust? Not at all! Paul anticipates the objection. If God chooses sovereignly, is He unjust? The answer: absolutely not. God's justice is not defined by human standards.

For He says to Moses, I will have mercy on whom I have mercy, and I will have compassion on whom I have compassion (Exodus 33:19). God's mercy is sovereign. He is not obligated to show mercy to anyone. Mercy, by definition, is undeserved. God's freedom to show mercy does not make Him unjust.

It does not, therefore, depend on human desire or effort, but on God's mercy. Salvation is not by human willing (desire) or running (effort). It depends entirely on God's mercy. This demolishes all boasting. Grace is sovereign.

For Scripture says to Pharaoh: I raised you up for this very purpose, that I might display my power in you and that my name might be proclaimed in all the earth (Exodus 9:16). God raised up Pharaoh for His own purposes. Even Pharaoh's hardness served God's plan to display His power and proclaim His name. God is sovereign over all, even His enemies.

Therefore God has mercy on whom He wants to have mercy, and He hardens whom He wants to harden. God's sovereignty includes mercy and hardening. Hardening is judicial: God gives people over to their rebellion (1:24, 26, 28). This is not arbitrary but just. God is free to show mercy or to harden.

One of you will say to me: Then why does God still blame us? For who is able to resist His will? The objection: If God hardens, how can He hold people accountable? If His will is irresistible, how is anyone responsible? This is the tension between sovereignty and responsibility.

But who are you, a human being, to talk back to God? Shall what is formed say to the one who formed it, Why did you make me like this? (Isaiah 29:16, 45:9). Paul does not answer the objection; he rebukes it. Creatures have no right to question the Creator. The clay does not interrogate the potter. God's ways are beyond human judgment.

Does not the potter have the right to make out of the same lump of clay some pottery for special purposes and some for common use? The potter analogy: God has absolute rights over His creation. He makes vessels for honor and dishonor from the same lump. This is not unjust; it is sovereign prerogative.

What if God, although choosing to show His wrath and make His power known, bore with great patience the objects of His wrath, prepared for destruction? Vessels of wrath are prepared for destruction. God endures them with patience, delaying judgment. His patience displays His longsuffering. Yet judgment will come.

What if He did this to make the riches of His glory known to the objects of His mercy, whom He prepared in advance for glory. Vessels of mercy are prepared in advance for glory. God's glory is displayed in both judgment (wrath) and salvation (mercy). Both reveal His character. Mercy shines brighter against the backdrop of wrath.

Even us, whom He also called, not only from the Jews but also from the Gentiles? The application: we (believers) are vessels of mercy, called from both Jews and Gentiles. God's election includes Gentiles, not just Jews. This expands the covenant people.

As He says in Hosea: I will call them my people who are not my people; and I will call her my loved one who is not my loved one (Hosea 2:23). Hosea prophesied the inclusion of Gentiles. Those not my people will become my people. God's love extends beyond ethnic Israel.

And, In the very place where it was said to them, You are not my people, there they will be called children of the living God (Hosea 1:10). The transformation: from not my people to children of the living God. This is the miracle of grace. Gentiles are adopted into God's family.

Isaiah cries out concerning Israel: Though the number of the Israelites be like the sand by the sea, only the remnant will be saved (Isaiah 10:22). Not all ethnic Israel will be saved, only a remnant. The remnant theme runs throughout Scripture. God preserves a faithful few.
For the Lord will carry out His sentence on earth with speed and finality (Isaiah 10:23). God's judgment is certain and swift. His word will be fulfilled. The sentence is final; there is no appeal.

It is just as Isaiah said previously: Unless the Lord Almighty had left us descendants, we would have become like Sodom, we would have been like Gomorrah (Isaiah 1:9). Without God's preserving grace, Israel would have been destroyed like Sodom and Gomorrah. The remnant exists because of God's mercy, not Israel's merit.

What then shall we say? That the Gentiles, who did not pursue righteousness, have obtained it, a righteousness that is by faith. The irony: Gentiles, not seeking righteousness, found it. They received righteousness by faith, not works.

But the people of Israel, who pursued the law as the way of righteousness, have not attained their goal. The tragedy: Israel pursued righteousness through law but failed. Effort without faith does not save. Legalism misses the goal.

Why not? Because they pursued it not by faith but as if it were by works. They stumbled over the stumbling stone. Israel's failure was pursuing righteousness by works instead of faith. The stumbling stone is Christ. He offends those who trust in their own righteousness.

As it is written: See, I lay in Zion a stone that causes people to stumble and a rock that makes them fall, and the one who believes in Him will never be put to shame (Isaiah 8:14, 28:16). Christ is both stumbling stone and cornerstone. To those who reject Him, He is an obstacle. To those who believe, He is the foundation. Faith in Him guarantees no shame (no disappointment). Christ divides: those who trust Him are saved; those who reject Him fall.
